content.text: Azure divides all operations against its services into two planes: the control plane and the data plane. Understanding this distinction is fundamental to designing correct RBAC policies, configuring audit logging, and understanding how different clients (CLI, SDK, Portal) authenticate to Azure. A failure to understand the plane distinction is one of the most common causes of misconfigured access control — for example, granting a team Contributor access (control plane) without realizing they still cannot read database records or storage blobs (data plane).

attrs.alt: Azure control plane vs data plane architecture

content.text: The Control Plane: Azure Resource Manager

The control plane is the management layer of Azure. All operations that create, configure, delete, or query Azure resource metadata go through Azure Resource Manager (ARM), accessible at https://management.azure.com. The ARM endpoint authenticates callers using Entra ID tokens scoped to the ARM audience. Every control plane operation — whether performed through the Azure Portal, az CLI, PowerShell, Terraform, Bicep, or a direct ARM REST API call — follows the same authentication and authorization path: Entra ID issues a token, ARM validates it, checks the RBAC role assignment, and either allows or denies the operation.
ARM authentication audience:
https://management.azure.com — the token issued for this audience works against management.azure.com only, not against storage endpoints, SQL servers, or Key Vault.
RBAC enforcement:
ARM enforces RBAC on every control plane request. An identity without a role assignment on the target resource or its parent scope receives a 403 Forbidden from ARM.
Azure Activity Log:

every ARM operation (successful or denied) is recorded in the Azure Activity Log with the caller identity, operation name, timestamp, and result. The Activity Log is the authoritative audit trail for control plane access.
Control plane does not touch data:
ARM can tell you that a storage account exists, what its configuration is, and who has access — but it cannot read the blobs inside. Reading blob data is a data plane operation.

# All control plane operations go through ARM at management.azure.com

# Create a storage account (control plane: ARM creates the resource)
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myStorage \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --location eastus

# This call goes to: POST https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/
#   resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myStorage?api-version=2023-01-01
# Authorization: Bearer token from Entra ID with ARM audience (https://management.azure.com)

# Assign RBAC role (control plane operation)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --assignee myapp@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv)

# View Activity Log for control plane operations (create, delete, RBAC, config changes)
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --start-time '2026-06-29T00:00:00Z' \
  --query "[].{caller:caller, operation:operationName.value, status:status.value, time:eventTimestamp}" \
  --output table

# Direct ARM REST API call (what the CLI calls under the hood)
ACCESS_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://management.azure.com --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts?api-version=2023-01-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" | python3 -m json.tool
The Data Plane: Service-Specific Endpoints

The data plane covers operations that read or write the actual content managed by an Azure service. Each service has its own data plane endpoint, authentication mechanism, and authorization model. Azure Blob Storage data plane requests go to https://<accountname>.blob.core.windows.net; Azure Key Vault secrets are accessed at https://<vaultname>.vault.azure.net; Azure SQL is accessed via TCP port 1433 to <servername>.database.windows.net; Event Hubs uses AMQP on port 5671/5672 to <namespace>.servicebus.windows.net.
Service
Data plane endpoint
Auth options
Data plane RBAC role (read)
Azure Blob Storage
https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net
Entra ID token (audience: storage.azure.com), SAS, access key
Storage Blob Data Reader
Azure Data Lake Gen2
https://<account>.dfs.core.windows.net
Entra ID token, SAS, access key, POSIX ACLs
Storage Blob Data Reader
Azure Files
SMB: //<account>.file.core.windows.net/<share>
Storage access key, Kerberos (AD DS or Entra ID)
Storage File Data SMB Share Reader
Azure Key Vault
https://<vault>.vault.azure.net
Entra ID token (audience: vault.azure.net)
Key Vault Secrets User
Azure SQL Database
<server>.database.windows.net:1433
SQL login, Entra ID user/service principal, managed identity
Database-level SQL roles (db_datareader)
Azure Cosmos DB
https://<account>.documents.azure.com
Entra ID token, primary key, resource token
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader
Event Hubs
<namespace>.servicebus.windows.net (AMQP/HTTPS)
Entra ID token, SAS policy connection string
Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver
Service Bus
<namespace>.servicebus.windows.net (AMQP/HTTPS)
Entra ID token, SAS policy connection string
Azure Service Bus Data Receiver

# Data plane operations go to service-specific endpoints, not management.azure.com

# Storage data plane: upload a blob (different endpoint, different auth)
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name myStorage \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --name data.csv \
  --file ./data.csv \
  --auth-mode login

# This call goes to: PUT https://myStorage.blob.core.windows.net/mycontainer/data.csv
# Authorization: Bearer token from Entra ID with Storage audience (https://storage.azure.com)
# OR: SAS token (account/service/user delegation SAS)
# OR: Storage access key (deprecated for identity-based access)

# Get a data plane access token for Azure Storage
STORAGE_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://storage.azure.com --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Upload using the raw REST API (data plane)
curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://myStorage.blob.core.windows.net/mycontainer/data.csv" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $STORAGE_TOKEN" \
  -H "x-ms-version: 2020-08-04" \
  -H "x-ms-blob-type: BlockBlob" \
  -H "Content-Type: text/csv" \
  --data-binary @data.csv

# Key Vault data plane: read a secret (different endpoint, different audience)
KV_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://vault.azure.net --query accessToken -o tsv)

curl -s -X GET \
  "https://myKeyVault.vault.azure.net/secrets/mySecret?api-version=7.4" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KV_TOKEN"

# Event Hubs data plane: send an event (AMQP or HTTPS)
# Python SDK uses the data plane endpoint:
# myEventHubNS.servicebus.windows.net (not management.azure.com)
Separate RBAC for Control and Data Planes

Control plane RBAC and data plane RBAC are separate systems with different role sets. The Owner and Contributor roles grant control plane access (can manage resources, read configurations, assign roles) but do NOT grant data plane access. This design is intentional: a DevOps engineer managing storage account configurations should not automatically have access to the data stored in that account, and a billing admin should be able to see resource costs without being able to read database records.

A user with Owner on a subscription can delete all storage accounts — but cannot read a single blob without also being assigned Storage Blob Data Reader or Storage Blob Data Owner. The control plane RBAC and data plane RBAC are completely independent access control systems.

# Control plane and data plane require separate RBAC roles
# A user with Contributor (control plane) CANNOT read blobs without Storage Blob Data Reader (data plane)

# Contributor role: can create/manage storage accounts but CANNOT read blob data
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee devops@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG

# Storage Blob Data Reader: can read blobs but CANNOT manage the storage account
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --assignee app@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv)

# Key Vault: 'Key Vault Contributor' manages the vault (control plane)
#            but CANNOT read secrets without 'Key Vault Secrets User' (data plane)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Key Vault Contributor' \
  --assignee admin@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --assignee app@contoso.com \
  --scope $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv)

# SQL: 'Contributor' manages the SQL server (control plane)
#       but SQL users/logins control data plane access (separate mechanism)
# SQL data plane access requires either:
# - SQL login (username/password inside SQL engine)
# - Azure AD user added to SQL database with CREATE USER [user] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER

# Audit: list data plane role assignments for a storage account
az role assignment list \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv) \
  --query "[?starts_with(roleDefinitionName,'Storage')].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  --output table
Audit Logging: Two Separate Log Streams

Control plane and data plane operations are logged separately. The Azure Activity Log captures all ARM (control plane) operations automatically with no configuration required — it retains 90 days by default and can be exported to Log Analytics for longer retention. Data plane audit logs are service-specific diagnostic logs that must be explicitly enabled per resource. For Azure Storage, data plane logs capture every blob read, write, and delete operation with caller IP, identity, and object path. For Key Vault, AuditEvent logs capture every secret, key, and certificate access. These logs are separate from the Activity Log and must be configured by the customer.

# Control plane and data plane have separate audit log streams

# Control plane log: Azure Activity Log
# Captures: create, delete, update, assign RBAC — all ARM operations
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --start-time '2026-06-29T00:00:00Z' \
  --query "[?contains(operationName.value,'write') || contains(operationName.value,'delete')].{caller:caller, op:operationName.value, status:status.value, time:eventTimestamp}" \
  --output table

# Data plane log: resource diagnostic logs (must be enabled per resource)
# For storage: StorageRead, StorageWrite, StorageDelete
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv) \
  --name storage-data-plane-logs \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --logs '[{"category":"StorageRead","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageWrite","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageDelete","enabled":true}]'

# For Key Vault: AuditEvent (captures every secret/key/cert access)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv) \
  --name kv-data-plane-logs \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --logs '[{"category":"AuditEvent","enabled":true}]'

# Query Log Analytics: who accessed Key Vault secrets in the last 24 hours
az monitor log-analytics query \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --analytics-query 'AzureDiagnostics | where ResourceType == "VAULTS" | where OperationName == "SecretGet" | project TimeGenerated, CallerIPAddress, identity_claim_upn_s, requestUri_s | order by TimeGenerated desc | limit 50' \
  --timespan P1D
Practical Implications
The control plane / data plane distinction has several practical consequences for architecture and operations:
Scenario
Implication
Application reads blobs from Storage
Needs Storage Blob Data Reader (data plane role), NOT Contributor
Terraform provisions storage accounts
Needs Contributor or Storage Account Contributor (control plane role) — no data plane role needed
App Service reads secrets from Key Vault
Assign managed identity Key Vault Secrets User (data plane role); Key Vault Contributor is not enough
SQL Server admin manages database schema
Needs Azure RBAC role to manage the server resource + SQL server-level login inside the database engine (two separate auth layers)
Audit: who uploaded files to Storage
Enable StorageWrite diagnostic logs on the storage account; Activity Log does not capture blob-level operations
Deny data access without removing management access
Remove data plane role assignment; Contributor can still manage the resource config but cannot read data
Block public internet access to data plane
Use Private Endpoint (data plane traffic) and service firewall rules; these are separate from ARM access which always uses management.azure.com

Private Endpoint applies to the data plane only. A storage account with a private endpoint and public access disabled still accepts control plane calls via management.azure.com (since ARM traffic is separate from data traffic). If you need to restrict control plane access to specific networks, use Azure Policy to deny management operations from outside approved IPs, or use management groups and locks.
Managed Identities: Bridging Control and Data Planes

Managed identities are the recommended mechanism for applications running on Azure to authenticate to data plane services without storing credentials in code or configuration. The identity is assigned to an Azure compute resource (VM, App Service, Function, AKS pod, Container Instance) and Azure automatically issues and rotates its credentials. The application code requests a token from the local Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint at http://169.254.169.254 — the token is scoped to a specific Azure service audience (storage, vault, SQL) and used to call that service's data plane API. The identity's data plane access is controlled by Azure RBAC role assignments.

# Managed identity: authenticate to data plane without credentials
# The identity is managed by Azure — no client secret or certificate to manage

# Enable system-assigned managed identity on an App Service
az webapp identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp

# Get the principal ID of the managed identity
APP_MI=$(az webapp identity show -g myRG -n myWebApp --query principalId -o tsv)

# Grant the App Service data plane access to read blobs
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --assignee $APP_MI \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv)

# Grant the App Service data plane access to read Key Vault secrets
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --assignee $APP_MI \
  --scope $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv)

# In application code (Python), use DefaultAzureCredential to get data plane tokens
# The credential detects the managed identity automatically in Azure environments

# DefaultAzureCredential acquires tokens for the data plane without hardcoded creds
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# The managed identity token is acquired automatically when running on Azure
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Access Azure Blob Storage data plane
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://myStorage.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential  # acquires token with audience: https://storage.azure.com
)

container_client = blob_client.get_container_client('mycontainer')
blobs = list(container_client.list_blobs())
print(f'Found {len(blobs)} blobs')

# Download a blob
blob_data = blob_client.get_blob_client('mycontainer', 'data.csv').download_blob().readall()

# Access Key Vault data plane (different endpoint, different audience token)
kv_client = SecretClient(
    vault_url='https://myKeyVault.vault.azure.net',
    credential=credential  # acquires token with audience: https://vault.azure.net
)

db_password = kv_client.get_secret('db-password').value

# Note: DefaultAzureCredential tries several auth methods in order:
# 1. EnvironmentCredential (AZURE_CLIENT_ID + AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET + AZURE_TENANT_ID)
# 2. WorkloadIdentityCredential (Kubernetes with federated identity)
# 3. ManagedIdentityCredential (VMs, App Service, Functions, AKS pods)
# 4. AzureCliCredential (developer machine running 'az login')
# 5. VisualStudioCodeCredential
# This means the same code works locally (uses CLI credentials) and in production (uses MI)
Infrastructure as Code and the Control Plane

Infrastructure as Code tools (Terraform, Bicep, ARM templates, Pulumi) operate exclusively on the control plane. They call ARM (management.azure.com) to provision and configure Azure resources. The service principal or managed identity used by the IaC tool needs control plane RBAC roles (typically Contributor at the subscription or resource group scope) to create and manage resources. Data plane roles are only needed if the IaC deployment also seeds data — for example, uploading initial blobs, pre-populating configuration secrets in Key Vault, or creating database schemas.

# Infrastructure as Code uses the control plane exclusively
# Terraform (and Bicep/ARM) call ARM to create/manage resources

# Terraform provider authentication (control plane access)
# terraform.tf:
# provider "azurerm" {
#   features {}
#   subscription_id = var.subscription_id
#   # Authenticates via managed identity (CI/CD), service principal, or az login
# }

# The Terraform service principal needs control plane roles:
# - Contributor (to create/manage most resources)
# - User Access Administrator (if Terraform manages RBAC assignments)

# Create a service principal for Terraform (control plane only)
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name terraform-sp \
  --role Contributor \
  --scopes /subscriptions/<subId>

# Terraform does NOT need data plane roles to create resources
# Example: az_storage_account in Terraform creates the account via ARM
# It does NOT need Storage Blob Data Reader to create the container resource
# (creating a container is a control plane op: PUT to management.azure.com)

# However, seeding data (uploading blobs via azurerm_storage_blob resource)
# DOES require data plane access (Storage Blob Data Contributor)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' \
  --assignee $(az ad sp show --id terraform-sp --query id -o tsv) \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv)
ARM deployment: always control plane.
Creating a storage account, container, or database via ARM does not require reading or writing the service's data.
azurerm_storage_blob (Terraform):
uploading a blob IS a data plane operation. The Terraform service principal needs Storage Blob Data Contributor on the storage account in addition to Contributor on the resource group.
Key Vault secret provisioning:
setting secrets in Key Vault via azurerm_key_vault_secret is a data plane operation requiring Key Vault Secrets Officer, not just Key Vault Contributor.
Least privilege for CI/CD:

use separate service principals for IaC (control plane: Contributor) and for application runtime (data plane: Storage Blob Data Reader, Key Vault Secrets User). Never give the IaC service principal data plane admin access.
Network Isolation: Control Plane vs Data Plane

Private Endpoint restricts access to the data plane of a service — it places a private IP address in the customer's VNet that routes to the service's data plane. This means blob uploads, database queries, and Key Vault secret reads must come from within the VNet (or via a connected on-premises network). However, the control plane (ARM calls to management.azure.com) is not affected by Private Endpoint — it always traverses the public internet. This is by design: ARM is a global service that must be reachable from any location where administrators operate.

# Network isolation applies differently to control vs data plane

# Private Endpoint: isolates the DATA PLANE from the public internet
# Storage with private endpoint: blob.core.windows.net still resolves
# but only within the VNet (via Private DNS zone override)

az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name storagePrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet AppSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id blob \
  --connection-name storage-connection

# Private DNS zone override (so myStorage.blob.core.windows.net resolves to private IP)
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name 'privatelink.blob.core.windows.net'

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name 'privatelink.blob.core.windows.net' \
  --name storageDNSLink \
  --virtual-network myVNet \
  --registration-enabled false

# Disable public data plane access (force data plane to go through Private Endpoint)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myStorage \
  --public-network-access Disabled

# NOTE: management.azure.com (control plane) is NOT affected by Private Endpoint
# ARM calls always go over the internet to management.azure.com
# Private Endpoint only applies to the service data plane endpoint
Network control
Affects control plane?
Affects data plane?
Notes
Private Endpoint on storage/SQL/KV
No
Yes
Data goes through private IP; ARM still uses management.azure.com
Service firewall (IP allowlist on storage)
No
Yes
Restricts data plane access by source IP; ARM unaffected
VNet Service Endpoint
No
Yes
Extends VNet identity to service; data plane accepts VNet traffic
NSG on VM's NIC
Partially (blocks outbound to management.azure.com if rule exists)
Yes (blocks outbound to service endpoint)
NSG controls all outbound traffic from the VM
Azure Firewall in hub VNet
Affects if UDR forces ARM traffic through firewall
Yes
Must create FQDN rule allowing management.azure.com for IaC VMs
Public access disabled on storage
No
Yes — only private endpoint or service endpoint traffic accepted
ARM still accepts control plane calls; only data traffic blocked
Azure SQL: Two Layers of Authentication

Azure SQL illustrates the control plane / data plane distinction particularly clearly because it has two completely independent authentication layers. The Azure Resource Manager control plane manages the SQL server resource (creating, scaling, configuring firewall rules, backups). The SQL engine itself — accessible via TCP 1433 — manages database-level authentication: SQL logins (username/password inside the engine), contained database users, and Entra ID users. A user with Contributor on the Azure subscription can create and delete SQL servers but cannot execute a single SELECT query without also being granted access inside the SQL engine's own auth layer.

Adding a user to the SQL Server's 'Entra ID admin' via the Azure portal is a control plane operation. Creating a contained database user inside the SQL database with CREATE USER...FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER is a data plane operation performed through the SQL engine. Both steps are required for Entra ID-based SQL data plane access.

# Azure SQL has two independent auth layers: ARM (control plane) + SQL engine (data plane)

# Control plane: create the Azure SQL server and database (ARM)
az sql server create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySQLServer \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password 'MySecure!Pass1'

az sql db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --name myDatabase \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --compute-model Serverless

# Control plane: set Entra ID admin for the SQL server (enables AAD auth to data plane)
az sql server ad-admin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server-name mySQLServer \
  --display-name 'SQL Admins Group' \
  --object-id $(az ad group show --group 'SQL Admins' --query id -o tsv)

# Data plane: create a contained database user from an Entra ID identity
# (run inside SQL — this is the SQL engine's own auth layer, separate from ARM RBAC)
# Connect as the Entra ID admin, then run:
# CREATE USER [myWebApp] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER;
# ALTER ROLE db_datareader ADD MEMBER [myWebApp];
# ALTER ROLE db_datawriter ADD MEMBER [myWebApp];

# Disable SQL authentication (force Entra ID only for data plane)
az sql server update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySQLServer \
  --enable-ad-only-auth true

# Application code: connect to SQL using managed identity (data plane)
# Connection string: Server=mySQLServer.database.windows.net;Database=myDatabase;
#                    Authentication=Active Directory Default;
# No username or password — the managed identity provides the token automatically
Cosmos DB: Migrating from Key-Based to RBAC Data Plane

Cosmos DB historically used account primary and secondary keys for data plane access — these keys grant full administrative access to all data in the account. Modern workloads should use Entra ID RBAC for Cosmos DB data plane access instead, which allows fine-grained role assignments (read-only, read-write, specific containers). The Cosmos DB built-in data roles are separate from Azure RBAC roles — they are Cosmos DB's own role system for data plane operations. Disabling key-based metadata write access ensures that data operations must use Entra ID credentials, not the master key.

# Cosmos DB: control plane vs data plane access

# Control plane: create Cosmos DB account (ARM)
az cosmosdb create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCosmosDB \
  --kind GlobalDocumentDB \
  --default-consistency-level Session \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0

# Control plane: create database and container
az cosmosdb sql database create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name myCosmosDB \
  --name myDatabase

az cosmosdb sql container create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name myCosmosDB \
  --database-name myDatabase \
  --name myContainer \
  --partition-key-path /customerId

# Data plane (traditional): use account primary key for CRUD operations
KEY=$(az cosmosdb keys list -g myRG -n myCosmosDB --query primaryMasterKey -o tsv)
# AVOID: primary key has full admin access to all data in the account

# Data plane (preferred): use Entra ID RBAC for data plane (requires enabling)
az cosmosdb update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCosmosDB \
  --disable-key-based-metadata-write-access true

# Assign Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader role (data plane only)
APP_MI=$(az webapp identity show -g myRG -n myWebApp --query principalId -o tsv)
COSMOS_SCOPE=$(az cosmosdb show -g myRG -n myCosmosDB --query id -o tsv)

az cosmosdb sql role assignment create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name myCosmosDB \
  --role-definition-name 'Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader' \
  --principal-id $APP_MI \
  --scope $COSMOS_SCOPE
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader:
can read all items and execute queries. Cannot write, delete, or modify container throughput.
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor:
can read, create, update, and delete items. Cannot manage the account configuration (throughput, indexing policy, etc.).
Custom roles:
can be created to restrict access to specific databases or containers within an account using scope paths like /dbs/<database>/colls/<container>.
Key rotation:
even when RBAC is used for new applications, the master key may still be needed for legacy tools. Enable automatic key rotation via Key Vault + Event Grid trigger.
Summary

The control plane is Azure Resource Manager (ARM) at https://management.azure.com — it handles creating, configuring, and deleting Azure resources. All control plane operations are authenticated with Entra ID tokens scoped to the ARM audience and authorized via Azure RBAC. Every control plane operation is logged in the Azure Activity Log automatically. The data plane is service-specific: each service has its own endpoint (blob.core.windows.net, vault.azure.net, database.windows.net), its own authentication audience, and its own RBAC roles. Control plane roles (Owner, Contributor) do not grant data plane access — separate data plane roles (Storage Blob Data Reader, Key Vault Secrets User, db_datareader) are required. Data plane audit logs are resource diagnostic logs that must be explicitly enabled per service. The separation enables principle of least privilege: infrastructure teams can manage resources without accessing data; applications can read data without modifying resource configurations.
